title: Ostomy Care: Colostomy and Ileostomy
subtitle: The nurse cares for an ostomy — assessing a healthy pink, moist stoma, emptying the pouch at 1/3-1/2 full, cutting the wafer to fit, protecting peristomal skin, and reporting a dusky or black stoma.

patient.name: Ostomy Care
patient.admitDate: The nurse assesses the stoma, changes the appliance, and protects peristomal skin.

nursesNote: The nurse provides ostomy care and teaching. A healthy stoma is pink to red, moist, and slightly raised; mild bleeding when cleaning is normal, but a dusky, purple, or black stoma signals impaired circulation and is reported promptly, as is retraction or excoriated, weeping peristomal skin. The pouch is emptied when it is about one-third to one-half full to prevent leaks, and the appliance is changed every 3-7 days or when leaking, ideally when output is least. The nurse measures the stoma and cuts the wafer about 1/8 inch larger so it protects the skin without constricting the stoma, and re-measures over the first 6-8 weeks as the stoma shrinks. Peristomal skin is cleaned with water and patted dry, since skin breakdown is the most common complication. Ileostomy output is liquid and enzyme-rich (higher dehydration and skin-injury risk), while a distal colostomy produces more formed stool. The nurse encourages self-care and supports body image.
providerOrders: Assess stoma color and report a dusky, purple, or black stoma
Empty the pouch when it is 1/3 to 1/2 full
Measure the stoma and cut the wafer ~1/8 inch larger
Protect and monitor the peristomal skin
Monitor ileostomy output for dehydration and teach self-care

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about ostomy care?

opts.text: A healthy stoma is pink to red, moist, and slightly raised
A dusky, purple, or black stoma should be reported
The pouch is emptied when it is about 1/3 to 1/2 full
A black stoma is a normal, expected finding
The wafer opening is cut slightly larger than the stoma
Mild bleeding when cleaning the stoma can be normal
Protecting the peristomal skin helps prevent breakdown
Ileostomy output is liquid and increases dehydration risk
The stoma shrinks over the first several weeks and is re-measured
The pouch should be left until completely full to save supplies
The appliance is best changed when output is least
A descending or sigmoid colostomy has more formed stool
Gather supplies and empty the pouch
Gently remove the old wafer and clean the skin
Assess the stoma and surrounding skin
Measure the stoma and cut the wafer ~1/8 inch larger
Apply the new wafer and pouch, pressing to seal
Pink, moist, slightly raised stoma
Dusky purple or black stoma
A small amount of bleeding when cleaning
Stoma retracting below skin level
Liquid output from a new ileostomy
No output with distension and cramping
Slightly swollen stoma in the first weeks
Excoriated, weeping peristomal skin

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The stoma now appears dusky and purple
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recognize this as a possible circulation problem
Assess the stoma color, moisture, and surrounding area
Notify the provider or surgeon promptly
Document the finding
Continue to monitor the stoma
Emptying the pouch at 1/3 to 1/2 full
Cutting the wafer opening much larger than the stoma
Reporting a dusky, purple stoma
Leaving leaking output on the peristomal skin
Cleaning peristomal skin with water and patting dry
Ignoring high liquid ileostomy output and signs of dehydration
Re-measuring the stoma as it shrinks over weeks
Waiting until the pouch is completely full to empty it
